label: fiction
title: The Borrowed Badge
setting: a small warehouse reception desk, the present day
elements: A parcel left the warehouse without authority
The accused took it dishonestly, knowing it was not theirs
hook: A late parcel vanished from a quiet reception desk. The night guard says he saw the trainee take it. The trainee says he only moved it to the safe shelf — and that the badge on the camera was already out of date.

accused.human: Nia Cole, 24. Three months into the night shift. Lives with her brother; this job pays the rent.
accused.if_guilty: a criminal record that ends the warehouse job and most others like it

prosecution.speaker: pc
prosecution.text: Members of the jury, this is a short case about a missing parcel and a badge on camera. You will see the trainee at the desk when the parcel disappears. You will hear that she had no authority to take it. That is enough to convict if you are sure she took it dishonestly.

defence.speaker: dc
defence.text: Nia Cole moved a parcel to the safe shelf, as she had been told to do with late drops. The badge on the camera feed was expired. The person who truly left with the parcel used that dead badge. If you are not sure it was her, you must acquit.

prosecution.speaker: pc
prosecution.text: The camera places her at the desk. The parcel is gone. The night guard saw her carry it away. Do not let a story about an expired badge erase what your eyes and ears have already heard.

defence.speaker: dc
defence.text: Place and time are not the whole charge. Dishonesty is. The expired badge and the safe-shelf policy leave real doubt. If she may have been doing her job, you must find her not guilty.

epilogue: Two weeks later a contractor was caught selling warehouse parcels from a van two towns over. His lanyard still held the expired badge from the camera. Nia kept her job. The warehouse changed how it retires old passes — and how it trains new night staff on the safe shelf.

beats.speaker: pc
beats.text: Exhibit one is the reception camera. At 1:12 in the morning a figure in a trainee jacket lifts a brown parcel from the desk and walks toward the side door. The on-screen name badge reads Nia Cole. The time stamp matches the missing-parcel log.
beats.reveal_note: The jacket and desk time looked damning, but the badge overlay was stale system text — not proof of who wore the pass that night.

beats.speaker: w1
beats.text: I was on the mezzanine. I saw the trainee take the parcel off the desk and head for the side door. Nobody else was in reception. Late parcels stay on that desk until morning unless a supervisor signs them out.
beats.reveal_note: Honest observation of movement — not of motive. He did not see the safe shelf or the door camera outside.

beats.speaker: w1
beats.text: 'How far were you from the desk?' 'Maybe twenty metres, looking down.' 'Could you read the badge with your own eyes?' 'No — I used the camera name on the monitor.' 'And you did not see the safe shelf by the side door?' 'Not from where I stood.'

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: How far were you from the desk?

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: Could you read the badge with your own eyes?

turns.speaker: w1
turns.text: No — I used the camera name on the monitor.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: And you did not see the safe shelf by the side door?

beats.reveal_note: He admitted the badge ID came from the monitor, not from seeing her face up close.

beats.speaker: clerk
beats.text: Exhibit two is the access log. The badge number printed on the camera overlay was retired three weeks earlier. The same number opens the side door at 1:14 — but the live staff system shows that number was already marked lost, not issued to the trainee.
beats.reveal_note: The decisive record: the badge on screen was dead inventory. Someone else used a retired pass while the camera still painted her name.

beats.speaker: acc
beats.text: My supervisor told me late drops go on the safe shelf by the side door, not the open desk. I moved that parcel there. I did not take it outside. I still had my own live badge on my lanyard when they searched me.
beats.reveal_note: Her account matched the safe-shelf policy and the live badge still on her — consistent with the access log.

beats.speaker: acc
beats.text: 'Why not call your supervisor before moving it?' 'Night trainees were told not to wake them for ordinary late drops.' 'So you chose to move it alone?' 'Yes — to the shelf I was shown on day one.'

turns.speaker: pc
turns.text: Why not call your supervisor before moving it?

turns.speaker: acc
turns.text: Night trainees were told not to wake them for ordinary late drops.

turns.speaker: pc
turns.text: So you chose to move it alone?

turns.speaker: acc
turns.text: Yes — to the shelf I was shown on day one.

beats.reveal_note: Acting alone sounds suspicious until you hear the written night-shift rule she followed.

beats.speaker: judge
beats.text: Members of the jury, the prosecution must prove each element beyond reasonable doubt. Suspicion is not enough. If you find yourselves unsure whether she took the parcel dishonestly, you must return a verdict of not guilty.
beats.reveal_note: The legal threshold — doubt about dishonesty means acquittal.

twist: The camera badge was a ghost label from a retired pass. The trainee moved the parcel to the safe shelf; a contractor with the dead badge took it from there.

jurors.label: Juror 2
jurors.persona: Takes the access logs at face value — her key, her laptop, her passphrase.

agree: That is what I have been saying — her key signed it, end of story.
See, even the machines agree with me.
pushback: You can spin the details however you like; the transfer still came from her chair.
Nobody walked in and borrowed her hands.
concede: Fine — if the caller was a fake, the login proves less than I thought.
final: I go with what the logs say.

jurors.label: Juror 3
jurors.persona: Practical; trusts what was written down and what was found.

agree: The records line up; I will follow the records.
That report is the most solid thing we have heard.
pushback: We keep talking feelings. Show me a document.
The policy existed and she skipped it — that is on paper too.
concede: The device download is a document as well. I have to weigh it.
final: On the papers alone, I can live with either answer — but sure is a high bar.

jurors.label: Juror 4
jurors.persona: The principled holdout; keeps repeating what 'sure' is supposed to mean.

agree: Precisely. The standard is sure — not suspicious, not probably.
pushback: You are asking her to explain the crime. That is not her job, it is the prosecution's.
Anger at an employer is evidence of nothing but employment.
concede: I accept the transfer itself is beyond dispute.
holdout: If a manufactured call is even reasonably possible, then sure is out of reach, and so is guilty.
I would rather be the last vote in the room than the first to guess.
final: Not guilty. That is what the doubt is for.

jurors.label: Juror 5
jurors.persona: Keeps the room honest about who has to prove what.

agree: That is the right way round: they prove it, she proves nothing.
pushback: Careless and dishonest are different accusations. Only one of them is charged.
We cannot convict her for being alone in the office.
concede: I lean guilty on the whole picture, but I will not pretend the burden moved.
burden_correct: Stop. She does not have to show where the money went. They have to show she sent it dishonestly.
The judge said it plainly: if she may genuinely have believed the caller, we acquit.
final: Held to the proper standard, I know which way this goes.

jurors.label: Juror 6
jurors.persona: Foreperson; keeps order, counts hands, leans to convict.

agree: Noted — and for what it is worth, I agree with that.
Good. That is the kind of point a verdict can stand on.
pushback: One at a time. The question is dishonesty, not tidiness.
We are going in circles; someone bring us back to the evidence.
concede: All right. That point stands, and the count should reflect it.
final: We have argued it properly. Let us be counted.

jurors.label: Juror 7
jurors.persona: Impressed by the username trail; drifts with whoever spoke last.

agree: Yes — that is the bit I keep coming back to as well.
When you put it like that, it does seem clear.
pushback: But the account had her name on it. Her actual old name.
Eleven days of planning sounds like somebody who works there.
concede: Although… if the name came from a leak, anyone could have worn it. I had not thought of it as a costume.
final: I think… I will go with how it sounded once everything was said.

jurors.label: Juror 8
jurors.persona: Came in certain; the platform report is working on him.

agree: I keep rereading that trust-and-safety line. A three-day-old synthetic account is not an accident.
If a crew ran that call live, then everything I was sure about happens exactly the same whether she is guilty or innocent.
pushback: The seven per cent bothers me less than you want it to.
I was certain when I sat down. I am telling you what certainty was built on: the login. Just the login.
concede: All right — I cannot call it sure anymore. Not with that report on the table.
final: I changed my mind in this room, and I can tell you the exact exhibit that did it.

jurors.label: Juror 9
jurors.persona: Quiet doubter; distrusts certainty offered too easily, from either side.

agree: Mm. The louder the witness, the less I tend to believe.
That is the first thing said today that sounded like evidence.
pushback: The colleague heard a complaint, not a confession.
Everyone here keeps saying 'obviously'. Nothing about this is obvious.
concede: The expert took money from the defence; I hold that against her a little.
final: Quietly, then: not sure. So not guilty.

jurors.label: Juror 10
jurors.persona: Wants the accused to account for herself; keeps reversing the burden.

agree: Exactly. Money gone, story ready — too neat by half.
pushback: Then where did the money go? Someone in this room tell me that.
burden_drift: If she is innocent, let her show us the caller was fake. She was the only one on that call.
An honest person rings the boss back. She never explained why she did not.
concede: All right, all right — the judge did say it is not hers to prove.
burden_correct: I withdraw it. They have to make us sure; she owes us nothing.
final: I want to say guilty. Wanting is not the standard, though, is it.

jurors.label: Juror 11
jurors.persona: The undecided one; argues both sides out loud until one sticks.

agree: Yes — say that part again, that is the part that moves me.
Right, right — the wallets. The wallets are hard to argue with.
pushback: But then I flip it around and it reads the other way entirely.
Five minutes ago I was where you are; give me five more.
concede: That one lands. I will stop wobbling on it.
final: I have talked myself around the table twice. This is where I stopped.

jurors.label: Juror 12
jurors.persona: Uneasy about convicting on screens; uneasy about acquitting on them too.

agree: That is fair. The wallets lead somewhere, and it is not to her.
And the direction backs that reading — sure means sure.
pushback: A machine flagged the video and a machine logged her key. I refuse to worship one machine and ignore the other.
Both sides keep handing me printouts and calling them proof.
concede: The device download cuts through it, I admit. You cannot fake an absence that thorough.
final: Between two stacks of screens, I take the one the judge's direction points at.
